# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation

Date: 2026-05-28

Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
roadmap entry.

---

## The plan

RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:

- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.

A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
requirement, which is also the natural paywall.

---

## Two models, two tiers

| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
|---|---|---|
| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
| GCP organization | not required | required |
| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |

Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
model-blind.

---

## The organization requirement is the tier boundary

Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.

An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
verify a domain.

---

## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products

GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
authenticating?*

| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
|---|---|---|
| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |

RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.

---

## How federation authenticates

The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
**never runtime**.

**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
roster.

**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
(RFC 8628):

1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
   is not involved.
3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.

**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**

```
POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
```

Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.

**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
long session.

---

## IdP landscape

Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.

---

## Refresh-token policy

A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
Confine it to exactly one place:

- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
  token.
- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
  outside the operator mode enum.

A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.

---

## Where configuration lives

Three homes, by scope:

- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
  independently straddle modes.
- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.

There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
secret.

---

## Operator-admin verbs

The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
on the operator's `principal://` subject.

Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.

---

## One consumer path, real and synthetic

Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.

---

## The single code seam

Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —

- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.

Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.

Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
again.

**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
the federation branch.**

---

## Open at implementation time

- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
  → what the token resolver consumes).
- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
  calls.

---

## Sources

- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)

-| Mode | File | Holds a secret? | Count |
-| `keyfile` (MVP) | **RBRA**, per-role | Yes (SA key) | 3 (gov/dir/ret) |
-| `federation` | **RBRI** (identity), per-operator | **No** (`external_account` config) | 1 |
+## The single code seam
-Under R2's direct-grant model there is nothing role-specific to store locally: a
-single `external_account` descriptor (pool, provider, audience, and a
-`credential_source`) is all that persists, holding no secret. Which of
-governor/director/retriever an operator may exercise is the set of IAM grants on
-their `principal://` subject, held **depot-side** — not encoded in any file. This
-supersedes memo-20260522 §6's "RBRA = impersonate-`director-SA` pointer": with
-impersonation gone (R2), the pointer degenerates to "which IdP/pool do I
-authenticate against," shared across every role the one operator holds.
-(`RBRI` appears free in the `RBR*` regime tree; a formal mint happens at
-implementation time.)
+Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
+token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —
-**One RBRI type serves both real and synthetic humans.** The schema is identical;
-the only difference is the `credential_source` field:
+- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
+- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.
-| Consumer | `credential_source` resolves against… | Persisted secret? |
-| Real human | a transient session cache, refreshed by interactive device flow | None |
-| Synthetic-human | an executable/file backed by a persisted refresh token | the refresh token, **separate from RBRI** |
+Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
+adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.
-This sameness is the *point*, not a convenience: §8 requires the synthetic human
-to walk the **same code path** as a real human, or CI validates the wrong thing.
-A shared RBRI type and an identical **consumer** path (read RBRI → resolve token →
-call GCP, never knowing what is behind it) guarantee that fidelity. The two
-diverge only in the **enrollment** verb (shipped device-flow vs test-only
-persisted-refresh), never in the file or the consumer. And RBRI stays **secretless
-in both cases** — the synthetic-human's durable secret lives in a separate
-quarantined artifact RBRI merely points at, so a leaked RBRI is inert.
+Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
+pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
+enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
+call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
+second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
+again.
-**Cult verbs: taxonomy invariant, bodies detox.** Across the keyfile→federation
-migration the verb register survives intact — **mantle** (Payor→Governor),
-**invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever), **divest**, **roster** keep their names,
-actors, and directions; users' vocabulary does not churn. Only the verb *bodies*
-change: from "mint SA + download key into RBRA" to "add/remove an IAM binding on
-the `principal://` subject." Federation implies exactly one register *addition*
-that keyfile mode has no equivalent of: a one-time **Payor-side federation-setup**
-verb (configure the workforce pool + IdP trust). The test-rig "log a machine in
-for a month" is a separate **test-only enrollment** verb, never in the shipped
-user register.
+**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
+the federation branch.**
-## 7. Deliberately left open
+## Open at implementation time
-These are implementation-tier and stay unanswered until the tier is chosen:
+- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
+- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
+  → what the token resolver consumes).
+- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
+  calls.
-- The exact regime home of the mode enum (station vs repo) and whether straddling
-  modes mid-migration is ever permitted.
-- Concrete `credential_source` wiring for the interactive-human case
-  (device-flow output → what GCP consumes).
-- The chosen IdP(s) and whether each offers headless device flow.
-- Coexistence/migration regime-file mechanics and the federation-setup verb's
-  signature and API calls.
## Sources

- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)

- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials

- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
-- [Identity federation: products and limitations](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/federated-identity-supported-services) — federated-identity access channels and per-product limits
-## Cross-references
-- `memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` — R1/R2/R3 (credential
-  model, tier ladder, deferral), R4 (synthetic-human CI + session policy), R5
-  (gauntlet coupling).
-- `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` — impersonation mechanism detail;
-  its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2.
-- RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry — the terse horizon record pointing here.

+- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
+- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)

lastPrompt: Yes, fold it into the memo: lets commit the memo to a 'both patterns' plan.  Make sure the memo doesn't contain a 'change log', I don't want to remember our prior concepts.  Proceed.

content.text:
Done. The memo is now a committed both-models plan, with no archaeology — nothing references prior framings, R-numbers, or superseded memos. It reads as the current concept only.

What it now says, in short:

- **Two permanent tiers**, selected per-depot by a mode enum: keyfile (free, no org) and federation (paid, org required). The org requirement is the paywall.
- **Mode enum + org-wide federation facts (pool/provider/audience) live in RBRD** — tamper-evident via the tripwire, and structurally enforcing one-mode-per-depot. Station scope holds only the per-machine credential source. **RBRA exists iff keyfile mode.** No separate per-operator credential file.
- **Confirmed in writing: federation operators use no RBRA.**
- **The single seam**: a role-keyed token accessor with the mode branch inside; downstream already model-blind. Sequencing spelled out — refactor the ~30 file-keyed call sites first (today, no behavior change), then add the federation branch.

Two things to note:

1. The **filename** still says `...-workforce-federation-feasibility.md`, which is now inaccurate (it's no longer feasibility, and it covers both models). I left it untouched because the RBSHR roadmap entry and other places likely point at this path. Say the word if you want me to rename it and chase the references.
2. I set the internal **date to 2026-05-28** (today, when committed); the filename keeps its original `20260527`.

The memo isn't committed to git yet — want me to notch it, and is this under a heat/pace I should affiliate it with?

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BR
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	This heat hardens RBK against GCP behaviors that surface only under real-world churn. Current content: one problem — **depot projectId reservation collisions**.
6	
7	GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after a project is deleted (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker allocator picks "next free" by walking a same-identity displayName search (`projects:search`, via depot_list). That search is identity-scoped: it returns the caller's own projects including their DELETE_REQUESTED ones — so **same-identity churn self-heals**, the allocator increments past its own pending-delete depots.
8	
9	The blindness is **cross-identity**: a projectId reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project is invisible to the caller's search. The allocator then derives a moniker whose global ID is already held elsewhere, and project creation fails with 409. This bit the project when the payor switched from the gmail (no-org) identity to the scaleinvariant identity: gmail's deleted `canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to the scaleinvariant allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor.
10	
11	## Scope and urgency
12	
13	Low urgency. Same-identity churn already self-heals; the residual collision recurs **only on a payor-identity switch** — rare. The session that found it worked around it with a family-stem bump. This is robustness, not a blocker.
14	
15	## Undecided (design, not yet settled)
16	
17	- **Where the fix lives.** The collider was the *test fixture* moniker picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, Rust); the production levy (`rbgp_depot_levy`) does not auto-pick — it uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. So "fixture-only Rust fix" vs "production levy change (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)" is an open choice — different surfaces, different blast radius.
18	- **What the fix does.** A candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404 free / 403 owned-elsewhere / 200 own; increment past taken) tests the real global constraint instead of the same-identity proxy — versus a lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance" approach.
19	
20	## Constraints
21	
22	- Any bash work here reads **BCG** (Bash Console Guide) first. `rbgp_Payor.sh` is a complex BCG-compliant module; do not write bash against it without the guide.
23	
24	## What done looks like
25	
26	A decision on fix surface (fixture vs production) and fix shape (probe vs guidance), then the implementation plus matching spec update. Until then, paces stay uncut.
27

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BR

## Shape

This heat hardens RBK against GCP behaviors that surface only under real-world churn. Current content: one problem — **depot projectId reservation collisions**.

GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after a project is deleted (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker allocator picks "next free" by walking a same-identity displayName search (`projects:search`, via depot_list). That search is identity-scoped: it returns the caller's own projects including their DELETE_REQUESTED ones — so **same-identity churn self-heals**, the allocator increments past its own pending-delete depots.

The blindness is **cross-identity**: a projectId reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project is invisible to the caller's search. The allocator then derives a moniker whose global ID is already held elsewhere, and project creation fails with 409. This bit the project when the payor switched from the gmail (no-org) identity to the scaleinvariant identity: gmail's deleted `canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to the scaleinvariant allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor.

## Scope and urgency

Low urgency. Same-identity churn already self-heals; the residual collision recurs **only on a payor-identity switch** — rare. The session that found it worked around it with a family-stem bump. This is robustness, not a blocker.

## Undecided (design, not yet settled)

- **Where the fix lives.** The collider was the *test fixture* moniker picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, Rust); the production levy (`rbgp_depot_levy`) does not auto-pick — it uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. So "fixture-only Rust fix" vs "production levy change (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)" is an open choice — different surfaces, different blast radius.
- **What the fix does.** A candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404 free / 403 owned-elsewhere / 200 own; increment past taken) tests the real global constraint instead of the same-identity proxy — versus a lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance" approach.

## Constraints

- Any bash work here reads **BCG** (Bash Console Guide) first. `rbgp_Payor.sh` is a complex BCG-compliant module; do not write bash against it without the guide.

## What done looks like

A decision on fix surface (fixture vs production) and fix shape (probe vs guidance), then the implementation plus matching spec update. Until then, paces stay uncut.

content.text: ₣BR's paddock is scoped to one specific problem (depot projectId collisions), not credentials — but the heat's theme, **rbk-16-gcp-hardening**, is the right umbrella: moving operators off SA-keys-on-disk to keyless federation is exactly GCP credential hardening. Per your delegation ("the heat you choose"), I'll notch the memo there now.

content.text:
Done. Working tree is clean — the commit captured all four changes (the earlier warning was benign; the rename landed as the old file's deletion + the new file's addition).

**Committed** (`1746b6e6`, affiliated to **₣BR rbk-16-gcp-hardening**):
- `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` — the renamed, rewritten both-models plan
- old `...-workforce-federation-feasibility.md` removed
- `RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc` + `memo-20260522-...reorientation.md` — references repointed, stale "RBRA→RBRI obsolescence" wording dropped

**One heat-shape flag before we go further.** I parked this on ₣BR because credential hardening fits its theme, but its paddock is currently a *single, unrelated* problem (depot projectId collisions, low-urgency). The credential-models work — this memo, the upcoming refactor, and the eventual federation build — is a distinct, larger thread. So when we slate the prep pace, you'll want to decide: **broaden ₣BR to carry credential hardening as a second problem**, or **nominate a dedicated heat** (e.g. `rbk-NN-operator-credential-models`) and transfer this commit's affiliation there. That's your call — it's a heat-shape decision.

---

Now, the **preparation step** for discussion. This is the one piece that's shippable *today*, keyfile-only, with zero behavior change:

> **Collapse the ~30 RBRA-file-keyed token calls behind a role-keyed accessor.**

Concretely: every site that today calls `rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}"` (and the governor/retriever variants) changes to ask for a token *by role* — e.g. a `token_for director` accessor. That accessor, in keyfile mode, just does what happens today (resolve role → RBRA file → JWT exchange). No federation code yet. The payoff: the RBRA-file concept stops leaking into 30 consumers, and later the federation branch slots into that one accessor without touching any call site again.

A few things worth deciding when we shape this pace:

1. **Where the accessor lives.** `rbgu_Utility.sh` already has the half-built shape (`rbgu_get_governor_token_capture`, `rbgu_authenticate_role_capture`) — natural home, but they'd need to become role-keyed and consistent.
2. **The role vocabulary.** Three roles (governor/director/retriever) → does the accessor take a role string, or stay three named functions? A string keeps the federation branch simpler later.
3. **Scope boundary.** The Payor path (`zrbgp_authenticate_capture`) and the cloud-build `rbgj*` token code stay *out* — confirm we're touching only the three operator roles.
4. **Verification.** It's a pure refactor, so the `service`-tier suite (the access probes exercise the keyfile token path) is the natural proof it's behavior-preserving.

Want to talk through any of these before we slate it — and which way are you leaning on the heat (broaden ₣BR vs. dedicated heat)?

message.content: Yes actually: I am musing that 'utility' is too generic a category- aren't there several diverse utilities there now?  Sometimes it is better to have smaller but more files.

lastPrompt: Yes actually: I am musing that 'utility' is too generic a category- aren't there several diverse utilities there now?  Sometimes it is better to have smaller but more files.

content: Another topic: do you see existing 'sprue' style role definitions in the codebase?  I think we incorporated, but I cannot remember.

content.text:
You're right, and the evidence backs the musing. `rbgu_Utility.sh` is 862 lines and 24 functions spanning at least four distinct concerns:

| Cluster | Functions (count) |
|---|---|
| **HTTP/REST transport** | `http_json`, `http_request`, `http_require_ok`, `http_json_lro_ok`, `poll_until_ok`, `poll_until_gone`, `newly_created_delay`, `http_code_capture`, `curl_status_is_transient_predicate` (~9) |
| **JSON/jq helpers** | `json_valid`, `json_field`, `write_vanilla_json`, `jq_file_to_file`, `error_message`, `jq_add_member_to_role` (~6) |
| **Credential/token/identity** | `get_governor_token`, `authenticate_role`, `rbra_load`, `rbro_load`, `extract_json_to_rbra`, `sa_email` (~6) |
| **GCP provisioning** | `api_enable`, `provision_service_agent` (2) |

So "Google Utility" has accreted into roughly four modules wearing one name. And the project already has precedent for the other direction — the handbook (RBH0) was decomposed one-function-per-file. Smaller, named files are the house style; rbgu is the outlier.

**Here's the part that matters for our task — it converges.** The role-keyed token accessor I floated for rbgu actually belongs to the *credential/token* cluster. And that cluster is precisely where the keyfile/federation fork will live. So extracting it isn't "while we're here" tidying — it's **load-bearing for federation**: it's the fork's home.

There's a wrinkle that makes the case stronger: the credential surface is *already* scattered across three modules —

- `rbgo` (OAuth) holds the keyfile token mint (`rbgo_get_token_capture` + the JWT build/exchange),
- `rbgp` (Payor) holds the Payor OAuth refresh,
- `rbgu` holds the thin wrappers (`authenticate_role`, `get_governor_token`) plus `rbra_load` / `rbro_load` / `extract_json_to_rbra`.

So the prep step is really **"gather the operator-credential surface into one focused module,"** not "add a function to rbgu." That module becomes the single seam — role accessor today, keyfile/federation fork tomorrow.

**The caution — don't let the musing swallow the pace.** The HTTP-transport and JSON clusters are *also* legitimate grab-bag candidates, but they're orthogonal to federation — mode-blind machinery that the token fork never touches. If we split everything at once, the federation prep drags in unrelated churn and risk. I'd keep those as a separate cleanup (their own pace, whenever), so the federation prep stays: *extract the credential/token module + introduce the role accessor.*

One mint note: a new module needs a deliberate prefix from the `rbg*` tree (e.g. `rbgt` Token / `rbgk` is free), decided properly rather than defaulting into rbgu.

So this sharpens the prep pace nicely — it's now "consolidate the credential surface into a focused module, with the role-keyed accessor as its entry point," which is both the de-grab-bagging you want *and* the federation seam. 

Does that framing match what you're after — credential cluster now, HTTP/JSON grab-bag split deferred? And still open: broaden ₣BR vs. a dedicated heat for all this.

content.text: "Sprue" = a regime **enum-value** convention (a literal wire-level enum value), per BUS0. Let me read the convention and a concrete example.

content.content:
987	Bash Utility Environment Regime
988	
989	=== Regime Enum Sprue Convention
990	
991	{axrg_regime_s} across BUK and the kits layered on it (Recipe Bottle's
992	RBRV/RBRN families among them) declare enumerated {axrg_variable_s} through
993	`buv_enum_enroll`.
994	This convention governs how the *values* of those enumerations are named, so
995	that each value is discoverable from its enum family by a single grep.
996	
997	[[bus_enum_sprue]]
998	//axl_voices axt_enum_value
999	{bus_enum_sprue}::
1000	A regime enum value carried in prefix-disciplined form rather than as a bare
1001	English word.
1002	The value is a sprue in the MCM sense (`mcm_sprue`): a literal wire-level
1003	token appearing verbatim in regime assignment files, Cloud Build
1004	substitutions, build-info JSON, and shell/Python comparisons.
1005	Bare, the token is invisible to its enum family; a recognizable prefix makes
1006	it also an MCM inlay (`mcm_inlay`) — recognized by pattern, so a `grep` on
1007	the family prefix returns the whole cluster.
1008	+
1009	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`
1010	+
1011	* `<proj>` — project cipher (`rb` Recipe Bottle, `bu` BUK)
1012	* `<n>` — regime-namespace signal (`n`; `r` is taken by the regime-variable
1013	  family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
1014	* `<regime>` — regime family letter (`v` vessel, `n` node, `c` config,
1015	  `s` station, `e` environment)
1016	* `<e>` — enum-value signal
1017	* `_<value>` — the canonical value name
1018	+
1019	Family-sharing is deliberate, not accidental: vessel-mode and egress-mode
1020	both carry `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums share
1021	`rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is voiced alike by ENTRY, DNS, and ACCESS.
1022	Uniqueness lives in the full value plus its owning {axrg_variable}, not in
1023	the shared prefix — one grep per family returns the complete cluster.

file.content:
Bash Utility Environment Regime

=== Regime Enum Sprue Convention

{axrg_regime_s} across BUK and the kits layered on it (Recipe Bottle's
RBRV/RBRN families among them) declare enumerated {axrg_variable_s} through
`buv_enum_enroll`.
This convention governs how the *values* of those enumerations are named, so
that each value is discoverable from its enum family by a single grep.

[[bus_enum_sprue]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value
{bus_enum_sprue}::
A regime enum value carried in prefix-disciplined form rather than as a bare
English word.
The value is a sprue in the MCM sense (`mcm_sprue`): a literal wire-level
token appearing verbatim in regime assignment files, Cloud Build
substitutions, build-info JSON, and shell/Python comparisons.
Bare, the token is invisible to its enum family; a recognizable prefix makes
it also an MCM inlay (`mcm_inlay`) — recognized by pattern, so a `grep` on
the family prefix returns the whole cluster.
+
Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`
+
* `<proj>` — project cipher (`rb` Recipe Bottle, `bu` BUK)
* `<n>` — regime-namespace signal (`n`; `r` is taken by the regime-variable
  family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
* `<regime>` — regime family letter (`v` vessel, `n` node, `c` config,
  `s` station, `e` environment)
* `<e>` — enum-value signal
* `_<value>` — the canonical value name
+
Family-sharing is deliberate, not accidental: vessel-mode and egress-mode
both carry `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums share
`rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is voiced alike by ENTRY, DNS, and ACCESS.
Uniqueness lives in the full value plus its owning {axrg_variable}, not in
the shared prefix — one grep per family returns the complete cluster.

==== Target Enums

# project-intimate trampoline (z-launcher) is the sole authority for the name;
# it exports BURD_CONFIG_DIR (absolute), and we derive the basename here so
# tooling (qualify, tabtarget creation) refers to it without re-literaling.
BUBC_moorings_dir="${BURD_CONFIG_DIR##*/}"
BUBC_launchers_subdir="rbml_launchers"
BUBC_rbmn_nodes_subdir="rbmn_nodes"
BUBC_rbmu_users_subdir="rbmu_users"

# Platform identifiers (bunne_* — BURN node-regime enum sprue family).
# These values are the canonical OS-family identifiers used in BURN_PLATFORM
# enrollment and in per-tabtarget platform invariant assertions.
# BUBC_platforms_<family> tinder constants provide single source of truth so
# code refers to the identifier by family name rather than hardcoding the
# literal token at every comparison site.
BUBC_platforms_linux="bunne_linux"
BUBC_platforms_mac="bunne_mac"
BUBC_platforms_windows="bunne_windows"

# Windows OpenSSH layout — forward slashes throughout so identical strings
# work in PowerShell, terminal display, and icacls invocations.
BUBC_windows_sshd_config='C:/ProgramData/ssh/sshd_config'
BUBC_windows_admin_auth_keys='C:/ProgramData/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys'
BUBC_windows_ssh_port="22"
BUBC_windows_fw_rule_name="sshd"
BUBC_windows_fw_display_name="OpenSSH Server"

# Windows registry preconditions for unattended power-on posture.
# Operator-handbook step (BUSJHW Windows: Host Availability) sets these;
# bujb_invigilate_windows reads them. Single source of truth so the path
# the handbook tells the operator to set is the path invigilate queries.
# PowerShell-canonical form (HKLM:\ prefix, mixed case — registry is
# case-insensitive at the OS level so display case is purely cosmetic).
BUBC_windows_passwordless_path='HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device'
BUBC_windows_passwordless_value='DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion'
BUBC_windows_aoac_path='HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power'
BUBC_windows_aoac_value='PlatformAoAcOverride'

# eof

file.content:
==== Target Enums

Vessel regime (`rbnve_`):

* `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` — bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_graft`
* `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` — tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap`

Node regime (`rbnne_`):

* `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE` — disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_enabled`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_global` / `rbnne_allowlist`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → same `rbnne_*` set

BURN node regime (`bunne_`):

* `BURN_PLATFORM` — linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux` / `bunne_mac` / `bunne_windows`

==== Use-Site Classification

A bare-word grep on a value yields four classes; only two of them migrate.

. *Phase / concept references* — "the conjure phase", "the airgap build":
  name the workflow, not the enum value. *Leave bare.*
. *Enum-value references* — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals, and
  default-value defaults (`${z_mode:-conjure}`): the actual variable value.
  *Migrate.*
. *Identifier embeds* — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, sigils like
  `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like `ordain-conjure`: the
  word is part of a larger identifier. *Leave bare.*
. *Coupled derived strings* — the value drives a derived token living in
  resource-name space that must itself stay bare. *Leave the derived string
  bare; migrate only the dispatch that selects it.* Two confirmed witnesses:
  egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether` /
  `-airgap` fragments in pool paths), and vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes
  (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*`).
  Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace: the
  arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
  `-tether`.

==== RBRN_RUNTIME Exclusion

`RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker` / `podman`) is deliberately *excluded* — its value
stays bare.
It is consumed as the literal executable name
(`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`, `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`).
Adopting the convention would demand a permanent value→binary indirection
layer at every dispatch site — a runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability
of two values that `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks.
This is the one regime enum where the convention would cost more than it
returns; the exclusion is a decision, not an oversight.

=== Kit Regime Operations

[[bukro_kindle]]
//axvr_kindle
{bukro_kindle}::
Silent initialization of Bash Utility Kit regime state from
{axrg_assignment_s}.
Sets defaults, detects unexpected variables, builds derived structures.

lastPrompt: Another topic: do you see existing 'sprue' style role definitions in the codebase?  I think we incorporated, but I cannot remember.

content.text:
Found it, and I can clear up the memory. Two different things are in play, and you incorporated **one** of them.

**What "sprue" actually is** (BUS0 §"Regime Enum Sprue Convention"): a regime *enum value* carried as a prefix-disciplined literal token instead of a bare English word — so one grep on the family prefix returns the whole enum. Pattern `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`. The live examples are all regime **modes**:
- `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_graft`, `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap` (vessel)
- `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_enabled` / ... (node)
- `bunne_linux/mac/windows` (BURN platform)

**Roles are NOT sprue-style.** What you're remembering is the recent **`RBCC_role_*`** centralization (`rbcc_Constants.sh:59-64`):
```
RBCC_role_governor="governor"
RBCC_role_retriever="retriever"
RBCC_role_director="director"
RBCC_role_payor / _assay / _mason
```
That gives you single-grep discoverability via the `RBCC_role_` *constant name* — the sprue *spirit* — but the **values are still bare words** ("governor"), not sprue tokens like `rbnre_governor`. And bare-word case arms still exist at the edges: `governor)`/`director)`/`retriever)` in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`, `rbra_cli.sh`, `rbra_regime.sh`, plus the `RBRA_ROLE` regime enum whose values are bare. So roles got *constant-name* discipline, not *enum-value sprue* discipline.

**Why this matters for the work in flight** — two hooks:

1. **The federation mode enum is a textbook sprue candidate.** `keyfile | federation` is a new regime enum; by BUS0 it *should* be sprue-shaped. But note: the pattern's regime-family letters are `v/n/c/s/e` — there's **no letter for depot**. Since we're homing the enum in RBRD, you'd be minting a new depot regime-family letter (something like `rbnde_keyfile` / `rbnde_federation`). That's a real mint to flag before building.

2. **Roles sit in the same gray zone as `RBRN_RUNTIME`** — the one enum BUS0 *deliberately excludes* from sprue, because its value is consumed as a literal (the binary name). Role values are consumed literally too: `${RBCC_role_governor}` is a **directory name** (`${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/governor/`, `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:35`) and the `RBRA_ROLE` regime value. That's exactly BUS0's "class-4 coupled derived strings" case (`BUS0:1054`). So if the role-accessor work tempts you to sprue-ify roles, the spec's own logic says: probably leave the *value* bare (like `RBRN_RUNTIME`) and discipline only the dispatch — which is precisely what the `RBCC_role_*` constants already do.

Net: you incorporated centralized role *constants* (RBCC), not sprue-style role *values* — and BUS0 arguably says roles *shouldn't* become sprues anyway. The genuinely new sprue is the federation mode enum, which needs a depot regime-letter mint.

## Paces

### fixture-codegen-projection (₢BPAAG) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical projection over a single-homed group; mirrors the colophon shape against a different bash owner.

## Goal

Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their canonical bash owner via the shared primitive and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem); migrate manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name canonical author
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror

## What done looks like

Fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; consumers reference the generated names; Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

### scattered-const-canonicalize (₢BPAAH) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical projection over two now-single-homed groups; the bash-home prerequisite landed in the chivvied front pace.

## Goal

Project operation verbs and container roles to generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared primitive, retiring their hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Assumes operation verbs and container roles each have one canonical bash declaration — landed by the front bash-home pace, now in `rbcc_Constants.sh` (`RBCC_verb_*`, `RBCC_container_*`).
- Feed them through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + stem); migrate the manifest consumers onto the generated consts.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh` — canonical bash home for operation verbs (`RBCC_verb_*`) and container roles (`RBCC_container_*`)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — operation/container hand-mirror

## What done looks like

Operation-verb and container-role consts are generated `RBTDGC_*` from their bash home; the manifest hand-mirror is retired for these groups; Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

### codegen-build-wiring-and-diff-gate (₢BPAAF) [rough]

## Character

Touches qualify infrastructure and build ordering; subtle — the regenerate-vs-consume distinction is load-bearing. Closes out the drift-protection handover for the whole projected set.

## Goal

Wire generation into the build lifecycle so every generated const file is regenerated at the BUK-available layer before each Rust build, never regenerated inside the container, and a stale commit fails qualify loud — then retire the runtime drift check the gate replaces.

## Shape

- Regenerate all generated files (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, scattered groups) before every Rust build, at the layer where BUK is available.
- A build inside the minimal container consumes the committed artifacts and never regenerates (no BUK present there).
- Add a `git diff --exit-code` qualify gate so a stale committed artifact fails loud.
- Retire `rbtdrm_verify` here, once the gate is live — exact equality supersedes its drift role. Retiring verify likely strands `rbtdrm_required_colophons` (its only consumer); remove that map too if nothing else reads it.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbtw_workbench.sh` and the rbtd build tabtargets — Rust build entry
- `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh`, `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh`, `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` — qualify gates
- Any existing diff-gate around the `buz_emit_context`-generated markdown context — precedent for the regeneration wiring

## What done looks like

- Every generated artifact is regenerated pre-build outside the container; the container build consumes the commit; qualify fails on a deliberately-staled artifact; `rbtdrm_verify` is gone.

Lets think about higher level category consonance.  We don't need to get it precisely right, mind you, but I suspec the utilities are mostly not google cloud affiliated?

content.text:
You're right to challenge the category — and the breakdown confirms your suspicion. `rbg` means "Google," but most of what's in `rbgu` isn't Google at all.

## What's actually in there, by Google-coupling

| Cluster | Functions | Google-specific? |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP transport** | `http_request`, `http_json`, `http_require_ok`, `http_code_capture`, `curl_status_is_transient`, `poll_until_ok`, `poll_until_gone` | **No** — generic curl/retry/poll |
| ↳ GCP-flavored fringe | `http_json_lro_ok` (LRO), `newly_created_delay` (propagation) | Yes-ish |
| **JSON/string** | `json_valid`, `json_field`, `jq_file_to_file`, `urlencode` | **No** — generic |
| ↳ GCP-flavored fringe | `write_vanilla_json` (IAM policy), `jq_add_member_to_role` (IAM), `error_message` (`.error.message` shape) | Yes (IAM) |
| **Credential/identity** | `get_governor_token`, `authenticate_role`, `rbra_load`, `rbro_load`, `extract_json_to_rbra`, `sa_email` | **No** — RBK's *own* credential model |
| **GCP provisioning** | `api_enable`, `provision_service_agent` | **Yes** — genuinely Google |

Roughly **13 of ~24 are generic or RBK-credential**, not Google. Only a Google fringe (IAM-policy JSON, LRO/propagation, `api_enable`, `provision_service_agent`) truly earns the `g`.

## The headline: the credential cluster *must* leave "Google"

This is the sharp one. The credential/token cluster is exactly the part federation **de-Googles** — under federation it talks to Entra/Okta/STS, not Google's token endpoint. Housing it under `rbg` would be wrong the day you ship federation and force a rename. So the consonant home is by **domain** (these are *RBK credentials*), not by "what API it happens to call today." That makes extracting it both grab-bag cleanup *and* the honest federation foundation.

## Proposed homes — by what the code *is*

1. **Generic HTTP + JSON/string** → arguably rises to **BUK** (the bash-utility layer). Not Recipe-Bottle-specific, not Google-specific — just bash infra. Most consonant. *Cost:* today they're coupled to `RBCC_CURL_*` constants and `ZRBGU_` temp paths, so promoting them means decoupling first. (Cheaper alternative: keep them in rbk under a non-Google generic-utility prefix.)
2. **Credential / identity (mode-neutral)** → a **new top-level rbk category**, explicitly *not* `rbg`. The federation seam lives here. This is the load-bearing extraction.
3. **Google-cloud specifics** (`api_enable`, `provision_service_agent`, IAM-policy JSON, LRO/propagation/error-shape) → **stay `rbg`**. `rbgu` then either dissolves into these or keeps only genuine Google glue.

## Mint reality (why not to lock letters tonight)

The top-level `rb` namespace is crowded — from the acronym map the mnemonic letters are mostly taken (`rbr` regime, `rbf` foundry, `rbi` image, `rbg` google, `rbw` is the workbench colophon, `rbc`/`rbd` constants, `rbm` moorings, `rbn` nameplate…). So the consonant category names probably *can't* get the obvious letter, and a proper mint pass is needed to find genuinely-free ones. Given you said precision isn't needed yet, I'd hold the letters open and just commit to the **three-home shape** above.

---

So, threads for the next turn(s):
- **Granularity**: three homes, or fold HTTP+JSON+generic into one "utility" home and only split out credentials? (Credentials is the one that *must* move; the rest is optional tidiness.)
- **BUK vs rbk** for the generic helpers — promote to the shared layer, or keep local under a non-Google rbk prefix?
- Still parked (your call when ready): the roles-sprue *scope* (how far the sprue reaches — directory names / `RBRA_ROLE` / params), and which MVP heat hosts the explode-utility pace (the category decision shapes that pace).

What's your instinct on granularity — is credentials-only the must-move, with the rest a lighter cleanup?

content.text:
Verified the namespace. Here's what's free and the scheme I'd commit to.

**Free as identifier prefixes** (trailing-`_` check came back empty): `rba`, `rbi`, `rbu`, `rbx`, `rbz`. The `rbu` family has only 4 incidental hits in rbk/buk and **no `rbu_` family exists** — so it's clean (worth one final grep before we land it, but effectively free).

## The scheme

Your `rbu` instinct is right for the *utilities* — but I'd hold one cluster out of it, for the same reason we're leaving `rbg`:

| Cluster | Home | Why |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP transport | **`rbuh`** | generic utility |
| JSON/jq/string | **`rbuj`** | generic utility |
| Credential / token / identity | **`rba`** (Auth) | a *domain*, not a utility |
| Google-cloud specifics | **`rbg`** (stays) | genuinely Google |

The key call: **credentials don't go in `rbu`.** "Utility" would be the same category error as "Google," one letter over — credentials are RBK's identity *domain* (and the federation seam). Lumping the federation-bound code under "utility" would mislabel exactly the part that matters most. So it gets its own domain letter: `rba` = Auth. (Mild note: `rba_` reads near "RBAC" and near `RBRA` — but in use, `rba_token_for` is unambiguous. `rbi` (Identity) is the alternative, but it brushes the Image concept; I prefer `rba`.)

**Single-letter clusters, fewer tokens** — agreed. And I'd drop the now-redundant infix, since the prefix already says it: `rbgu_http_request` → `rbuh_request`, not `rbuh_http_request`.

## Why `rba` stays terminal (federation-safe)

You don't need an `rba*` family. The keyfile/federation fork lives as *functions inside* one `rba` module (the role accessor branches internally) — not as child modules. So `rba` can name the module today and just *grow functions* when federation lands in ₣BR. No children, no terminal-exclusivity conflict, no rename later. This MVP pace stays pure reorg; the fork itself is ₣BR's job.

## The full explosion (rbgu → 0)

| `rbgu_*` today | → | New home |
|---|---|---|
| `http_request`, `http_json`, `http_require_ok`, `http_code_capture`, `curl_status_is_transient`, `poll_until_ok`, `poll_until_gone` | → | **`rbuh`** |
| `json_valid`, `json_field`, `jq_file_to_file`, `urlencode`, `error_message` | → | **`rbuj`** |
| `get_governor_token`, `authenticate_role`, `rbra_load`, `rbro_load`, `extract_json_to_rbra`, `sa_email` | → | **`rba`** |
| `write_vanilla_json`, `jq_add_member_to_role` (IAM policy) | → | **`rbgi`** (IAM — they're Google) |
| `api_enable`, `provision_service_agent`, `http_json_lro_ok` (LRO), `newly_created_delay` (propagation) | → | **`rbg`** residue (Google glue) |

`rbgu` then **dissolves entirely** — no "utility" module wearing the Google letter survives.

Two judgment calls flagged for your eye: `error_message` (extracts GCP's `.error.message` shape — I put it in `rbuj` as a generic jq-extract, but it leans Google) and `sa_email` (Google-ish, but it's a credential-identity format helper, so I kept it with `rba`). Both could flip to `rbg` if you'd rather.

---

Does this match what you want — `rbuh`/`rbuj` for utilities, `rba` for the credential domain, Google glue back to `rbgi`/`rbg`? And do you want HTTP+JSON kept as two modules, or is that over-split (could be one `rbu`-something)?

My "rbg residue" was sloppy. The Google glue must land in specific children: IAM bits → `rbgi`; the GCP-call glue (LRO, propagation) wants its own named child rather than a bucket. So — agreed, specific child always, never bare `rbg`.

content.text:
Surveyed the family — here's the full roster so the mint is conflict-free:

`rbga` ArtifactRegistry · `rbgb` Buckets · `rbgc` Constants · `rbgd` DepotConstants · `rbgg` Governor · `rbgi` IAM · `rbgl` GarLayout · `rbgo` OAuth · `rbgp` Payor · `rbgv` AccessProbe · `rbgj*` cloud-build jobs (+ `rbgh`/`rbgr` Cloud-Build workflow fns). Free letters: **e, f, k, n, s**.

## Resolution: only one new mint needed

Most of the Google residue has an **existing** home — no new letter required:

| Functions | Home | Note |
|---|---|---|
| `write_vanilla_json`, `jq_add_member_to_role` (IAM policy) | **`rbgi`** | IAM — exact fit |
| `provision_service_agent` | **`rbgi`** | service agents are IAM identities |
| `error_message` (`.error.message`) | **`rbuj`** | generic jq-extract (leans GCP; flip to `rbge` if you'd rather) |

The only thing with **no existing home** is the GCP-call mechanics — the GCP-aware layer sitting *over* `rbuh`'s generic transport:

**New mint: `rbge` — "Recipe Bottle GCP REST"** (`rbge_GcpRest.sh`)
- `http_json_lro_ok` — long-running-operation polling (LRO is GCP's own term)
- `newly_created_delay` — propagation/eventual-consistency settling
- `api_enable` — serviceusage enable call

Charter is tight on purpose — "GCP-convention wrappers over `rbuh`" — so it doesn't drift back into a bucket.

**Why `rbge` not `rbgs`:** I considered `rbgs` ("Service"), but "Service" is exactly the kind of vague umbrella that invites grab-bag regrowth. The cluster's real essence is GCP *REST/operation mechanics*, not services — and the soft `e` mnemonic is carried by the filename (`rbge_GcpRest.sh`), consistent with how `rbgl`/`rbgv` already work.

Verified free (0 hits) and no concept overlap with any existing child.

---

So the complete scheme is now closed: `rbuh` / `rbuj` / `rba` / `rbge` (new) / `rbgi` (existing). The only thing still open is the roles-to-sprue pace (separate, likely ₣BM) — which we left for next session. Good place to stop for today?

message.content: I think we will stop once we have slated one or both these paces.  Any more issues to resolve before we do this?

content.text:
Two different answers for the two paces.

**Explode-`rbgu` (₣BS): clean — no blockers.** I just closed the last two open micro-calls so the mapping is fully locked:
- `error_message` → **`rbge`** (it parses GCP's `.error.message` envelope — belongs with the GCP-call glue, not the generic jq bucket)
- `sa_email` → **`rbgi`** (GCP SA-email format is IAM-adjacent; keeps `rba` purely RBK-credential, which is the federation-clean outcome)

Final mapping: `rbuh` {http_request, http_json, http_require_ok, http_code, curl-transient, poll_until_ok/gone} · `rbuj` {json_valid, json_field, jq_file_to_file, urlencode} · `rba` {get_governor_token, authenticate_role, rbra_load, rbro_load, extract_json_to_rbra} · `rbge`(new) {lro_ok, newly_created_delay, api_enable, error_message} · `rbgi` {write_vanilla_json, jq_add_member_to_role, provision_service_agent, sa_email}.

Everything left is *mechanical* and belongs in the docket as pointers, not blockers: the call-site sweep (rbgu is ~460 references), the per-module kindle/sentinel + sourcing re-wiring, and relocating the `ZRBGU_` temp-file machinery. Boundary is firm: the role accessor + keyfile/federation fork stays in ₣BR; this pace just moves and renames. Library-only — no tabtarget/colophon impact. Ready to slate.

**Roles-to-sprue (₣BM): one real issue to settle first.** The sprue *scope*. Your "it's an internal convention" rationale is sound for the **directory names** — but the role value also surfaces in a more operator-facing spot: it's a CLI folio (`rbra_cli.sh` BUZ_FOLIO role; the auth-regime render/validate tabtargets take role as a param). So the fork is:
- **Full sprue** — value becomes `rbnre_governor` *everywhere*, including that param, so an operator typing it types `rbnre_governor`.
- **Class-4** (BUS0's own coupled-derived-string pattern) — sprue the internal dispatch/comparisons, but keep the operator-typed param and the on-disk path bare via a deliberate derivation.

That decision shapes the docket, so it's worth one answer before slating that pace.

**Suggestion:** let me draft and slate the **explode-`rbgu`** pace into ₣BS now (it's fully resolved), and either settle the roles scope in one more exchange and slate it too, or leave roles for next session. Which do you want — both, or just the explode pace tonight?

input.content:
# jjezs_slate explode-rbgu-grab-bag

## Character
Intricate but mechanical — a behavior-preserving move/rename sweep.

## Goal
Dissolve `rbgu_Utility.sh` (the "Google Utility" grab-bag) into focused modules whose prefix matches what the code actually is. No behavior change.

Slated: **₢BSAAG (explode-rbgu-grab-bag)** in ₣BS rbk-10-mvp-modern-organization-features. The docket locks the full mapping, marks it behavior-preserving, and fences the federation fork off to ₣BR.

Now the second pace.

## 1. The "Class #" taxonomy — credit where it's due, it's *yours*

I should be honest: that's not a framework I brought — I'm quoting **BUS0's "Use-Site Classification"** (the subsection right under the Regime Enum Sprue Convention). I picked it up when I read the sprue spec earlier this session. So if it's been making good distinctions, that's your spec doing the work; I just had it freshly in context.

The taxonomy: a bare-word grep on an enum value yields **four classes of hit, and only two migrate to sprue**:

1. **Phase/concept reference** — "the conjure *phase*"; names the workflow, not the value → **leave bare**.
2. **Enum-value reference** — equality tests, `case` arms, JSON literals, `${x:-default}` defaults; the actual variable value → **migrate to sprue**.
3. **Identifier embed** — the word is *inside* a larger identifier (`RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, a sigil, a test-step label) → **leave bare**.
4. **Coupled derived string** — the value drives a *derived* token living in **resource-name space** that must itself stay bare (your witnesses: egress→worker-pool suffixes, vessel-mode→hallmark prefixes) → leave the derived string bare, **migrate only the dispatch that selects it**.

That's the lens I was applying to roles — asking, per site, "class-2 (migrate) or class-4 (coupled)?"

## 2. You're right — they're internal, so it's full sprue

And this resolves it cleanly under the taxonomy:

- The role words in **tabtarget names / doc filenames** (`OnboardingCredentialRetriever`, etc.) are **Class 3** — identifier embeds — so they stay bare *regardless*. Never touched.
- The role **value** in case arms, comparisons, `RBRA_ROLE`, the folio — **Class 2** — migrates to sprue.
- The key insight is exactly yours: the only "derived string" a role drives is the **secrets directory name**, and that is **RBK-internal filesystem layout, fully renameable** — *not* an external resource name like a GCP pool suffix. So it is **not Class 4**. The class-4 exclusion only protected strings pinned in *external* namespaces; roles have none. Full migration, grep-certainty for everything `*_governor`, distinct from vague bare words — confirmed.

## Two residuals to settle, then I slate it

1. **Does the sprue reach the on-disk directory?** Code-level certainty is free (sprue the value + route every case arm through it). But if the *directory* also becomes `rbnre_governor/`, existing installs need their secret dirs migrated. There's precedent — the `rbdc_DerivedConstants` migration block already renames role dirs — so it's a cheap shim, not a blocker. Full sprue (dir included, + shim) vs. dir-stays-bare-derived. Your "it's all internal" leans toward **dir included**; I'd agree, for filesystem-level greppability too.

2. **The sprue family letter needs a mint.** The pattern is `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`, but `v/n/c/s/e` (vessel/node/config/station/environment) don't cover roles. Roles live in the **auth** regime (`RBRA_ROLE`), so the natural new family letter is `a` → **`rbnae_governor`** / `rbnae_director` / `rbnae_retriever`. (Same kind of mint the depot mode-enum needs — a new regime letter.)

Give me a call on those two — dir-included (yes/no) and the `rbnae_` family letter — and I'll draft and slate the roles-sprue pace into ₣BM.

# jjezs_slate roles-to-sprue

## Character
Mechanical migration under a new enum family, plus one deliberate die-text breadcrumb.

## Goal
Migrate operator role enum values from bare words ("governor") to sprue tokens, so every role-value site is greppable as a family — closing the gap where the role family violates the Regime Enum Sprue Convention.

## The mint
New family **`rbnae_`** (rb · n · a=auth regime · e=enum-value): `rbnae_governor` / `rbnae_director` / `rbnae_retriever`, extended across the `RBCC_role_*` set. Register it in BUS0 "Target Enums" beside `rbnve_`/`rbnne_`/`bunne_`.

## Migrate — Class 2 only (per BUS0 Use-Site Classification)
- Make the sprue tokens the canonical values in `rbcc_Constants.sh` `RBCC_role_*`.
- Sweep enum-value sites onto them: `case` arms, equality tests, `RBRA_ROLE` value and its regime validation, folio defaults.
- Leave bare — Class 3 — role words embedded in tabtarget colophons/frontispieces and doc filenames.

## Secret directories
- Dir names derive from the const, so they become `rbnae_governor/` etc. automatically. Operators rename existing dirs by hand.
- No auto-repair: retire any existing role-dir migration dynamic. On a role-credential-not-found die, append a breadcrumb such as `" (have secrets directories been renamed correctly?)"`.

## Discovery
- `grep RBCC_role_` and bare `governor|director|retriever` across `Tools/rbk`; classify each hit against BUS0 Use-Site Classification.

## What done looks like
Role values are `rbnae_*` tokens; one family grep returns every role-value site; tabtarget/doc names unchanged; BUS0 lists the new family; the not-found die carries the rename breadcrumb; shellcheck + relevant suites green.
